title: Orlando Naturalization Lawyer | Florida Naturalization Services

## How to Become a U.S. Citizen in Florida
Are you a lawful permanent resident (green card holder) living in Florida? If so, you may be able to apply for U.S. citizenship through the naturalization process. Citizenship can bring many benefits, so it is well worth the effort. However, the naturalization process can be quite complex and confusing.
The good news is that you don’t have to go through it alone. Our Orlando immigration attorneys can guide you through every step of the process, providing the support and guidance you need. At Brown Immigration Law , we offer comprehensive naturalization services in Orlando, FL and surrounding areas.
Contact us today at (321) 701-2882 to learn more about how we can help you become a U.S. citizen in Florida.
Providing immigration solutions in Ohio, North Carolina, and Florida. Contact us today for a personalized consultation.

## Naturalization vs. Citizenship
Citizenship is a legal status that grants an individual certain rights and responsibilities within a specific country. It can be acquired in various ways, including birth, descent, marriage, or through naturalization. Citizens enjoy full legal protection and have the right to participate in the political and social life of the country.
Naturalization is the process through which an individual who is not initially a citizen of a country acquires citizenship later in life. This process typically involves fulfilling specific requirements, such as residency, language proficiency, and knowledge of the country’s history and government. After meeting these requirements, the individual may apply for naturalization, and if approved, they become a naturalized citizen.

## Eligibility for Naturalization in Florida
Before you can apply for U.S. citizenship in Florida, you must meet certain eligibility requirements.
To qualify for naturalization in Florida, you must:
- Be at least 18 years old
- Be a lawful permanent resident ( green card holder )
- Have resided in the U.S. as a green card holder for at least 5 years (or 3 years if you are married to a U.S. citizen)
- Have been physically present in the U.S. for at least half of the required residency period
- Have lived in the state or USCIS district where you are filing your application for at least 3 months
- Have lived in the U.S. continuously since filing your application
- Be a person of good moral character
- Demonstrate an attachment to the principles and ideals of the U.S. Constitution
- Be able to read, write, and speak basic English
- Be able to pass a U.S. history and government test
- Be willing to take an Oath of Allegiance to the United States
Keep in mind that there are certain exceptions to these requirements for certain qualified individuals. An immigration attorney can help you determine if you qualify for naturalization.

## How Long Does the Naturalization Process Take in Florida?
The naturalization process in Florida can take a considerable amount of time. In most cases, it takes around 5 to 6 months from the date you file your application to the date you are naturalized. However, the process can take longer if you have a complicated case or if there are errors on your application.
One of the best ways to ensure that your naturalization application is filed correctly and without errors is to work with an experienced immigration lawyer. At Brown Immigration Law, we can help you prepare and file your naturalization application in Florida, as well as help you prepare for your naturalization interview and test.

## How to Apply for Naturalization in Florida
Applying for naturalization in Florida is a two-step process. The first step is to prepare and file your application. The second step is to attend a naturalization interview and test.
Here is an overview of the naturalization process in Florida:
- Complete and file the naturalization application (Form N-400) with the USCIS . You will need to include your supporting documents and the required filing fee with your application.
- Wait for the USCIS to schedule your naturalization interview . You will receive a notice in the mail telling you when and where to appear for your interview.
- Attend your naturalization interview and bring your supporting documents . You will also be required to take the naturalization test at your interview. If you pass the test and interview, the USCIS will send you a notice stating that your application has been approved.
- Receive a notice in the mail telling you when and where to attend your naturalization ceremony . You will take the Oath of Allegiance to the United States and receive your Certificate of Naturalization at your ceremony.
If your application for naturalization in Florida is denied, you have the right to appeal the decision. An immigration attorney can help you file an appeal and represent you throughout the appeals process.
